plan.description:
In this document, the Data Management Plan (DMP) is going to be tackled to
describe the main elements of the data management policy among the consortium
in the context of the project research data. In this manner, the life cycle of data,
data types, and Intellectual Property Right (IPR) requirements are discussed in
further detail. This would include the generation, storage and update of the data
during the project along with the standards of sharing and reusing of such a data.
The DMP is going to be a living document where each partner among the project
consortium will update regularly the significant changes throughout the project
lifetime.
plan.title: FAIR-ification of Supplementary Data to Support Clinical Research

reference.label: University of Luxembourg - Luxembourg Centre for Systems Biomedicine (LCSB)
LISN Laboratoire Interdisciplinaire des Sciences du Numérique
Haute Ecole Spécialisée de la Suisse Occidentale

description.description: This is the updated version of Data Management Plan (DMP) for the FAIRClinical Project.
description.label: CHIST-ERA Data Management (2026)

descriptionTemplate.label: CHIST-ERA Data Management
descriptionTemplate.description: This is a DMP template created based on the CHIST-ERA RDM policy and DMP template.

page.title: Data Description And Collection Or Reuse of Existing Data

options.label: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)
options.value: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)

options.label: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
options.value: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)

options.label: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)
options.value: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)

fieldSet.title: Give details on the kind of data
fieldSet.description:
The main distinction between data types is between primary data and secondary data. Data collection may contain both primary and secondary data depending on the source where they have been derived from. 

  • primary data is data that have been collected for the first time and have not undergone through data processing and/or analysis, yet. 

  • secondary data is data that have been cleaned up, analysed and shared by others (published or unpublished) and they are those that are being typically reused. 

In addition, types of data unveil how they have been collected so they also depend on the process that led to their generation.

fieldSet.title: Give details on the data format
fieldSet.description:
Data may be found in different formats. Usually, outputs of specific instruments/ services/ tools where data are generated and/ or processed have a default way of exposing their outputs following specific standards. 

Most common distinctions between formats are proprietary and non proprietary formats, because they may impact data access in the long-term.

options.label: de facto standard in my discipline
options.value: de facto standard in my discipline

options.label: de iure standard in my discipline
options.value: de iure standard in my discipline

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Justify the use of certain formats
fieldSet.description: For example, decisions may be based on staff expertise within the host organisation, a preference for open formats, standards accepted by data repositories, widespread usage within the research community, or on the software or equipment that will be used.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Give preference to open and standard formats as they facilitate sharing and long-term re-use of data (several repositories provide lists of such ‘preferred formats’).

fieldSet.title: Give details on the volumes
fieldSet.description: Give measurable examples per type of output based on common practices in the field. These might be relevant to the volumes of data and how many bytes of storage they occupy, numbers of objects, files, rows, and columns.

section.title: What data (for example the kind, formats, and volumes), will be collected or produced?

referenceTypeData.label: Select the tools used for this activity

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Explain which methodologies or software will be used if new data are collected or produced or if third party data are used
Explain how data provenance will be documented

section.title: How will new data be collected or produced?
Documentation And Data Quality

fieldSet.title: Indicate which metadata will be provided to help others identify and discover the data
fieldSet.description:
Metadata is data about data and is an essential set of information describing scientific outputs, in the form of either physical or digital objects, in a machine-readable format. According to the expected use, metadata can be given different attributes. Most common type which enables discovery and identification are descriptive metadata. descriptive metadata contain information about key aspects needed to search for and successfully find a given scientific output, e.g. by its: 

  • title, 

  • author/creator, 

  • abstract, 

  • keywords

Moreover, metadata may be used for describing a service or a scientific instrument.

fieldSet.title: Indicate which metadata standards will be used
fieldSet.description: Use community metadata standards where these are in place.

fieldSet.title: Indicate how the data will be organised during the project
fieldSet.description: Νaming conventions should be clearly described in order to make data understandable by other researchers that may reuse them after the project ends. Μentioning for example conventions, version control, and folder structures. consistent, well-ordered research data will be easier to find, understand, and re-use.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Check out some examples of file naming best practices: http://tiny.cc/op8lcz

labelData.label: Please provide information and links to the documentation, should it is available

fieldSet.title: Consider what other documentation is needed to enable re-use
fieldSet.description: This may include information on the methodology used to collect the data, analytical and procedural information, definitions of variables, units of measurement, and so on.

section.description: For example the methodology of data collection and way of organizing data
section.title: What metadata and documentation will accompany the data?

options.label: To compare and combine with other data
options.value: To compare and combine with other data

options.label: To follow-up research on a specific area
options.value: To follow-up research on a specific area

options.label: To contribute to the wider community (researchers, public authorities, citizen scientists)
options.value: To contribute to the wider community (researchers, public authorities, citizen scientists)

labelData.label: Please provide URL/Location of the DMP where reused data are documented

fieldSet.title: How will existing data be re-used?
fieldSet.description: Apart from data produced within your research, you might want to repurpose data that have been produced and shared by others, in different research context. Ιf you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc.

fieldSet.title: Where can re-used data be found?
fieldSet.description: Select the repository/ies where data are hosted.

fieldSet.title: Which data will be re-used?
fieldSet.description: Select the datasets that will be reused. Make sure that you select the correct filenames and versions!

section.title: Reused Data

page.title: Storage And Backup During The Research Process

section.title: Storage And Backup During The Research Process

fieldSet.title: Explain how the data will be recovered in the event of an incident and describe the main risks and how these will be managed
fieldSet.description: Consider data protection, particularly if your data is sensitive for example containing personal data, politically sensitive information, or trade secrets.

section.title: How will data security and protection of sensitive data be taken care of during the research?

page.title: Legal And Ethical Requirements, Codes Of Conduct

section.title: Legal And Ethical Requirements, Codes Of Conduct

labelData.label: How will compliance with legislation on personal data and on security be ensured?

fieldSet.title: Are there any personal data to be formulated?
fieldSet.description: It is possible that data are sensitive, such as personal or confidential, and hence they can not be shared directly to others. For those types of data, specific processes need to be made in order to ensure their reliable exchange in the short and long-term.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find out more about sensitive data and how to process them: https://www.openaire.eu/sensitive-data-guide

fieldSet.title: Explain whether there is a managed access procedure in place for authorised users of personal data

section.description: Ensure that when dealing with personal data data protection laws (for example GDPR) are complied with: ›› Gain informed consent for preservation and/or sharing of personal data. ›› Consider anonymisation of personal data for preservation and/or sharing (truly anonymous data are no longer considered personal data). ›› Consider pseudonymisation of personal data (the main difference with anonymisation is that pseudonymisation is reversible). ›› Consider encryption which is seen as a special case of pseudonymisation (the encryption key must be stored separately from the data, for instance by a trusted third party).
section.title: Personal data
How will other legal issues, such as intellectual property rights and ownership, be managed? What legislation is applicable?

fieldSet.title: Who will be the owner(s) of the data?
fieldSet.description: Meaning who will have the rights to control access.

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer
Please give more information, including links to specific documents (if available)

fieldSet.title: Explain what access or restrictions will apply to the data?

section.description: Make sure to cover these matters of rights to control access to data for multi-partner projects and multiple data owners, in the consortium agreement.
section.title: Data ownership and accessibility

fieldSet.title: Explain which intellectual property and how will they be dealt with

section.title: Intellectual property rights

fieldSet.title: Are there any restrictions on the re-use of third-party data?

section.title: Third-party data restrictions

options.label: Legal issues such as IP rights
options.value: Legal issues such as IP rights

options.label: Environmental issues such as the locating of endangered species
options.value: Environmental issues such as the locating of endangered species

options.label: Security issues for organisations or countries
options.value: Security issues for organisations or countries

fieldSet.title: What ethical issues and codes of conduct are there, and how will they be taken into account?

section.description:
Consider whether ethical issues can affect how data are stored and transferred, who can see or use them, and how long they are kept. Demonstrate awareness of these aspects and respective planning. 
Follow the national and international codes of conducts and institutional ethical guidelines, and check if ethical review (for example by an ethics committee) is required for data collection in the research project.
section.title: Ethical issues
Data Sharing And Long-term Preservation

options.label: Deposit in a FAIR-enabling data repository
options.value: Deposit in a trustworthy data repository

options.label: Use of a secure data service
options.value: Use of a secure data service

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Explain how the data will be discoverable and shared
fieldSet.description: Please note that “shared” refers to sharing with people outside the Project consortium.

fieldSet.title: Outline the plan for data preservation and give information on how long the data will be retained
Explain when the data will be made available
Indicate the expected timely release
Will exclusive use of the data be claimed?
Indicate whether and for how long data sharing will be embargoed or restricted
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For example to publish, protect intellectual property, or seek patents.

fieldSet.title: Indicate how the data will be used
Is it necessary to restrict access to certain communities or to apply a data sharing agreement?
Explain how and why and what action will be taken to overcome or to minimise restrictions

section.description: Please check with funders' or institutions's policies which might apply an embargo on data access.
section.title: How and when will data be shared? Are there possible restrictions to data sharing or embargo reasons?

fieldSet.title: Indicate what data must be retained or destroyed for contractual, legal, or regulatory purposes
Indicate how it will be decided what data to keep
fieldSet.description: Decision trees for digital preservation: https://www.canada.ca/en/heritage-information-network/services/digital-preservation/decision-tree.html

options.label: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)
options.value: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)

options.label: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
options.value: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)

options.label: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)
options.value: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)

fieldSet.title: Describe the data to be preserved long-term
Indicate where the data will be deposited
fieldSet.description: Select a repository that will host the research data outputs for long-term preservation.

fieldSet.title: Indicate how the data will be shared
Indicate whether potential users need specific tools to access and (re-)use the data.

section.description: For example a data repository or archive.
section.title: How will data for preservation be selected, and where data will be preserved long-term?

fieldSet.title: What type of persistent identifier (PID) will be used?
fieldSet.description:
Persistent identifiers should be applied so that data can be reliably and efficiently located and referred to. Persistent identifiers also help to track citations and re-use. A persistent identifier or PID is a long-lasting reference to a resource. Whatever resource it refers to, the primary purpose of the PID is to provide the information required to reliably identify, verify and locate it. A PID may be connected to a set of metadata describing an item rather than to the item itself.
Typically, a trustworthy, long-term repository will provide a persistent identifier.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Learn more about PIDs and how to use them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-can-identifiers-improve-the-dissemination-of-your-research-outputs

section.title: How will the application of a unique and persistent identifier to each data set be ensured?
Data Management Responsibilities And Resources

fieldSet.title: Outline the roles and responsibilities for data management/stewardship activities
fieldSet.description: Name responsible individual(s) where possible.
fieldSet.extendedDescription:
For example:
  • data capture, 
  • metadata production, 
  • data quality, 
  • storage and backup, 
  • data archiving, and 
  • data sharing.

section.description: Provide names and responsibilities of researchers’ or data managers’ data management and stewardship activities that are performed throughout the project. Follow the research data management lifecycle to guide your steps in data capture, metadata production, data quality, storage and backup, data archiving, and data sharing.
section.title: Who will be responsible for data management?

fieldSet.title: Explain how the necessary resources to prepare the data for sharing/preservation have been costed in
fieldSet.description: Research Data Management is not a costless procedure, so it is important that it is thought through at the planning stage of research. Carefully consider and justify any resources needed to deliver the data. These may include storage costs, hardware, staff time, costs of preparing data for deposit, and repository charges.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To understand what costs are allocated across the steps of a RDM lifecycle, how to estimate them and how to prevent them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

fieldSet.title: Indicate whether additional resources will be needed to prepare data for deposit or to meet any charges from data repositories

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Explain how much cost provisionally is needed
fieldSet.description: You can use: Data Management Costing Tool (zingtree.com)

section.title: What resources will be dedicated to data management and ensuring that data will be FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Re-usable)?

reference.label: Haute Ecole Spécialisée de la Suisse Occidentale

field.textValue:
Multiple cloud systems are being used along with virtual machines
where the deposition of produced data will be distributed.

FairClinical is targeting secondary data of publicly open-accessed scientific publications where the aim specifically is to process supplementary data attached to such publications. Supplementary data files are provided by journal publishers in a range of formats, including PDFs, word processor documents (i.e., doc or docx), spreadsheets (i.e., xls or xlsx), images (i.e., jpg, gif, png, TIF, tiff), plain text files (i.e., txt, csv, xml, rtf, html), and movies. The BioC format has been used in FAIRClinical for the storage and exchange of a full-text corpus of published clinical case reports. Note that, movies have been excluded; however, as the technology to automatically transcribe audio from movie files improves, such files can be reconsidered in future versions of the corpus.
Search API
SIBiLS
Repository
Secondary data

textListValues.textListValue: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)

field.textValue: SIB Literature Services (SIBiLS)

Articles are output in a compressed archive file that uses the same naming convention as the input archive. Additionally, for each output archive, an article log is output in TSV format. The article log indexes all articles included in the archive and consists of the following fields:
  1. PMC identifier
  2. Article title
  3. Article subtitle (if the reason for inclusion)
Log files are created to list the supplementary files that are included and excluded for each archive being processed. The included file log lists the files that remain in the raw directories following the movie file removal process. The TSV formatted file contains the following fields:
  1. Supplementary data directory
  2. PMC identifier
  3. URL of the file
The excluded file log is also in TSV format and lists the movie and archive files removed, with the following fields:
  1. PMC identifier
  2. URL of the movie removed (field 3 is null) or URL of the archive if movie file removed (field 3 has a value) or URL of archive removed if empty after movie removal (field 3 is null)
  3. Filename of the movie removed from the archive in field 2
The enriched metadata of the clinical case report form will be deposited into the SIBiLS which will enable future researchers to search for such CRFs.
All data wil be retained and no need for destroying
Retained

Since the data is public and open-accessed hence, there will be no
restricted access.
Giving access to virtual machines, APIs and enabling deposition

textListValues.textListValue: To compare and combine with other data
To follow-up research on a specific area
To contribute to the wider community (researchers, public authorities, citizen scientists)

field.textValue: Section 1.2.2

textListValues.textListValue: Deposit in a trustworthy data repository

field.textValue: The software code and written documentation will be protected under copyright law, ensuring that ownership and distribution rights are maintained
None

Stage 1: PMC BioC full-text retrieval and processing
PubMed Central Open Access full-text articles are available in BioC JSON or BioC XML, in Unicode or ASCII, from a FTP server hosted by the National Centre for Biotechnology Information (NCBI). The articles are available in compressed archive files, grouped by PMC identifier. Each BioC JSON ASCII archive is downloaded programmatically from the FTP server (https://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pub/wilbur/BioC-PMC/), before being processed. Each archive is processed individually by the workflow. In an initial housekeeping step, the file extension for each file is changed from .xml to .json to accurately represent the file contents. Once renamed, the files are optimised for use with the BioC Python module ‘bioc’ (https://github.com/bionlplab/bioc) by expanding their parent JSON array structure so that only BioC JSON content is present.

Stage 2: Application of a search query
The search query filters articles based on the presence of the string “case report” within the article title. However, PMC splits some article titles into a title and a subtitle. In the BioC file, the title section text is queried for the presence of the “case report” string. If the string is not found and the subtitle parameter is present, the subtitle is also searched for the “case report” string. Some of the filtered PMC articles contain just a title or a title and abstract. In order to create a full-text corpus, these articles are excluded by scanning the final section type of the BioC file and removing those files where the final section type is “abstract” or “title”.  


Stage 3: Supplementary file retrieval and processing
Each article is scanned for BioC section types containing a value of “SUPPL”, indicating supplementary content is associated with the article. Since the links to external resources are not included in BioC files, they are retrieved from the HTML version of an article using a regular expression search. The supplementary files linked in an article are downloaded programmatically. For each article containing supplementary files, a new directory is created with the name <PMCID>_supplementary, where <PMCID> is the article’s PMC identifier. Within these directories, two subdirectories named raw and processed are created. The raw directory contains each original supplementary file downloaded for the article and the processed directory contains the output from the stage 4 file standardisation process. Due to the increased storage requirements for retaining movie files compared to other file types, movies are removed. The URLs for removed movie files are captured in an excluded log file so they can be retrieved if required. Each file extension is analysed and those with movie extensions (mp4, mov, avi, wmv, webm, flv, mpg, movi, m4v, 3gp) are expunged. Since movies can also be contained within archives and compressed archives, these are analysed and movies expunged. If the removal of movie files results in an empty archive (i.e. no other file types are contained in the archive) then the archive itself is expunged. Similarly, if the removal of movie files (and/or archives if necessary) results in the removal of all supplementary files associated with an article (i.e. only movie files are associated with an article), then the <PMCID>_supplementary directory is expunged.

Stage 4: Supplementary file standardisation
Existing methods for converting a variety of file types into computer interpretable formats are used and extended in the workflow. The Auto-CORPus python package converts HTML text content to a BioC JSON format and HTML tables to a BioC-like JSON format where column and row orders are preserved along with cell contents. The SIBiLS documents and annotations fetch API provides text extracted from images and a separate OCR web service processes unseen images to extract text. The Auto-CORPus package has been extended to process new file types:
PDF files are processed using the ‘marker-pdf’ Python module (https://github.com/VikParuchuri/marker) to extract text which is then converted to BioC JSON.
Word processor .doc and .docx files are processed with the ‘python-docx’ Python module (https://pypi.org/project/python-docx/) and the extracted text converted to BioC JSON.
Presentation .ppt, .pptx and .odp files are processed using the ‘python-pptx’ Python module (https://pypi.org/project/python-pptx/) to extract text from slides which is stored in BioC JSON.
Tables are extracted from spreadsheet .xls and .xlsx files, as well as from .csv and .tsv files, using the ‘pandas’ Python module (https://pandas.pydata.org/) to convert the loaded data frame object into a BioC-like tables JSON format.

Image .jpg, .png and .tiff files are initially queried using the SIBiLS fetch API (https://sibils.text-analytics.ch/api/fetch). If the image has previously been processed then the returned image text is stored in BioC JSON format. If the image has not previously been processed, then the image is sent to the SIBiLS OCR API (https://ocrweb.text-analytics.ch/) to generate new text which is structured and stored as BioC JSON.

The computer interpretable version of supplementary files are stored in the processed directory for each article, appending _bioc.json or _table.json to the filename. If there is an error during file processing, the file is added to the unprocessed file log. The TSV formatted file contains the following fields:
  1. Supplementary data directory
  2. PMC identifier
  3. Filename
  4. Error message

FairClinical repositories (i.e., GitHub, GitLab) would contain the source code
along with references of any other APIs or existing models that might be
involved.
No ethical issues
Clinical Case Report Forms (CRFs)

While researchers primarily access biomedical literature through web browsers that display HTML or PDF formats of publications, these formats are not optimized for natural language processing (NLP) and text mining. BioC is a simple data structure, in either XML or JSON format, to store and exchange text documents and annotations between different language processing systems and text mining tools. Digital archives of biomedical literature, such as PubMed Central (PMC) and SIB Literature Services (SIBiLS) provide BioC versions of the full text of publications to support text analytical activities. PMC makes articles available in BioC format from the PMC Open Access Subset and the PMC Author Manuscript Collection. The PMC BioC key file defines the semantics associated with the BioC data. In order to optimize the formats of supplementary files for NLP and text mining, they will be transformed to computer interpretable formats. The Auto-CORPus tool for converting biomedical literature to the BioC format has been extended to transform PDFs, word processor documents, spreadsheets, presentations, and text files to BioC. SIBiLS has extensively processed supplementary image files using optical character recognition (OCR) methods to extract text. These resources have been used to standardise the supplementary files associated with the clinical case report corpus to develop a clinical supplementary materials corpus.

SIBiLS is hosting the publications, whereas, university of Luxembourg will
host the OMOP output and the source code will be stored in github/gitlab.
By the end of the FairClinical project, the OMOP will be available.
No

Raw Supplementary Data Files: 31 GB
BioC corpus of the Supplementary Data Files: 18GB
